message.content:
Below are the **canonical, published sources and standards** you can safely cite when defining SEO and structured‑data testing criteria for web applications. I’ll focus on official docs only, with what they cover and their URLs.

---

### 1. Google Search Central – Core SEO Documentation

**Name:** Google Search Central  
**Organization:** Google  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

From the main documentation hub and SEO Starter Guide:  

- **Crawlability & indexing** – how Googlebot crawls, how to allow/block crawling (robots.txt), sitemaps, canonicalization, duplicate content handling, and indexing basics.[1][2]  
- **Site structure & navigation** – internal linking, hierarchical site structure, breadcrumbs.[1]  
- **On‑page SEO & metadata** – title elements, meta descriptions, headings, language declarations.[1]  
- **Canonicalization** – rel="canonical" usage, handling duplicate URLs, parameters.[1]  
- **Mobile‑friendliness** – mobile‑first indexing, responsive design guidance.[1]  
- **Page experience & performance signals** – Core Web Vitals, HTTPS, intrusive interstitials.[1]  
- **Structured data** – general guidelines and implementation practices for schema.org markup used by Google.[1]  
- **Image, video, and JavaScript SEO** – media optimization and JS rendering guidance.[1]  

**Key URL:**  
- Main portal: `https://developers.google.com/search`[2]  
- SEO Starter Guide: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide`[1]  

---

### 2. Google Search Console – Testing & Diagnostics

**Name:** Google Search Console  
**Organization:** Google  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

Search Console is the **canonical toolset** for testing how Google Search sees your site:[4]  

- **Index coverage & crawlability** – Index Coverage report, Crawl stats, URL Inspection tool (crawl, indexing, canonical, last crawl).[4]  
- **Enhancements / structured data eligibility** – dedicated reports for rich result types (e.g., Products, FAQ, Breadcrumbs), showing valid, warning, and error states in structured data.[4]  
- **Mobile‑friendliness & usability** – Mobile Usability and Page Experience reports (where available), surfacing issues that affect mobile users.[4]  
- **Sitemaps & canonicalization signals** – sitemaps submission, inspection of selected vs. user‑declared canonical.[4]  
- **Security & manual actions** – coverage of security issues and manual spam actions that affect visibility.[4]  

**Key URL:**  
- About Search Console: `https://search.google.com/search-console/about`[4]  

---

### 3. Google Rich Results & Structured Data Documentation

**Name:** Rich Results & Structured Data Documentation  
**Organization:** Google  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

This is the **canonical source for Google’s structured‑data eligibility criteria** for rich results.[1]  

- **Rich result types & eligibility requirements** – per‑feature docs (e.g., Article, Product, Recipe, FAQ, How‑to, Video, Events, JobPosting) defining:  
  - Required and recommended schema.org properties  
  - Content and quality guidelines  
  - Technical requirements (AMP where applicable, image sizes, etc.)[1]  
- **Structured data quality and spam policies** – global requirements for all structured data used in Google Search.[1]  
- **Testing & validation** – pointers to tools like the Rich Results Test and structured‑data reports in Search Console.[1][4]  

**Key URLs (entry points):**  
- Structured data: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data`[1]  
- Search Gallery (rich result types): `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance`[1]  

---

### 4. Google Rich Results Test

**Name:** Rich Results Test  
**Organization:** Google  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

The Rich Results Test validates structured data specifically for **Google Search rich results**:[1]  

- **Structured data syntax & parsing** – validation for JSON‑LD, Microdata, RDFa as used by Google.  
- **Feature‑specific eligibility** – checks whether markup qualifies for particular rich result types (e.g., Breadcrumb, Product, FAQ) based on Google’s rich result specs.[1]  
- **Mobile vs. desktop rendering** – lets you test with Google’s smartphone or desktop crawler to see render‑dependent markup.[1]  

**Key URL:**  
- Rich Results Test: `https://search.google.com/test/rich-results` (linked from Search Central structured‑data docs)[1]  

---

### 5. schema.org – Vocabulary Specification for Structured Data

**Name:** Schema.org  
**Organization:** Schema.org (collaborative initiative originally by Google, Microsoft, Yahoo, Yandex)  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

Schema.org is the **canonical vocabulary** for structured data used in SEO and rich results:[1]  

- **Types, properties, and enumerations** – definitions for entities like `Article`, `Product`, `Organization`, `BreadcrumbList`, etc.  
- **Global structured‑data modeling** – how to model content domain‑independently (events, local businesses, reviews, etc.).  
- **No search‑engine‑specific ranking or eligibility criteria** – it defines the vocabulary, not which markup search engines will use.  

**Key URL:**  
- Home & schema browser: `https://schema.org/`  

(Official Google usage of schema.org is documented in Search Central structured‑data pages above.[1])

---

### 6. Open Graph Protocol

**Name:** The Open Graph protocol  
**Organization:** Open Graph protocol  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

The **OGP** is the canonical standard for social preview metadata used by Facebook and many others:[1]  

- **Social sharing metadata** – properties such as `og:title`, `og:type`, `og:image`, `og:url`, `og:description`.  
- **Content types** – extension mechanisms for objects like articles, videos, music, etc.  
- **Preview card rendering semantics** – how shared URLs should be represented as objects on social platforms (title, description, image).  

**Key URL:**  
- Specification: `https://ogp.me/`  

---

### 7. Twitter Cards / X Cards

**Name:** Twitter Card Markup (Twitter/X Cards)  
**Organization:** Twitter (X Corp.)  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

Twitter/X Cards define how URLs expand inside Twitter/X clients:[1]  

- **Card types** – `summary`, `summary_large_image`, `player`, `app` cards with their specific markup requirements.  
- **Metadata properties** – `twitter:card`, `twitter:title`, `twitter:description`, `twitter:image`, and optional account and app fields.  
- **Validation eligibility** – criteria for a page to render a card, and how Twitter’s crawler reads card metadata.  

**Key URL:**  
- Cards overview & markup reference: `https://developer.twitter.com/en/docs/twitter-for-websites/cards/overview/abouts-cards` (Twitter Developer docs; path names sometimes change but this is the canonical developer domain)  

---

### 8. Lighthouse SEO Audits

**Name:** Lighthouse SEO audits  
**Organization:** Google (Chrome / web.dev)  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

Lighthouse (as documented on web.dev) defines a **standard set of automated SEO audits**:[1]  

Typical SEO audits include, for example:  

- **Crawlability / indexability basics** – checks for `robots.txt` availability and a valid HTTP status code.  
- **Metadata & HTML semantics** – presence of `<title>`, meta description, valid `hreflang`, document `<lang>`, `<meta viewport>`, and descriptive link text.  
- **Mobile‑friendliness baseline** – viewport configuration and tap targets (via accessibility and best‑practices audits, often referenced from SEO guidance).  
- **Structured data surface checks** – not full eligibility, but checks for structured‑data presence for some features where applicable.  

Lighthouse is not a ranking standard, but is widely treated as the **canonical automated checklist** from Google for baseline technical SEO readiness.  

**Key URL:**  
- SEO category docs on web.dev: `https://web.dev/lighthouse-seo/`  

---

### 9. W3C HTML & URL Standards (Supporting Technical Foundations)

These are not SEO‑specific but are **canonical for correct markup and parsing**, which underpins crawlability and structured‑data reliability.

#### a. HTML Living Standard / HTML 5

**Name:** HTML Standard  
**Organization:** WHATWG (used by browsers; referenced by W3C HTML specs)  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

- **Valid HTML structure** – elements, attributes, semantics that affect how search engines parse headings, links, metadata, and structured data containers.  
- **Metadata syntax** – `<head>`, `<meta>`, `<link rel=…>`, including canonical links, hreflang, and charset declarations.  

**Key URL:**  
- HTML Standard: `https://html.spec.whatwg.org/`  

#### b. URL / HTTP standards (for completeness)

**Name:** URL Standard & HTTP Specifications  
**Organizations:** WHATWG (URL), IETF (HTTP)  

**What they define for SEO‑relevant testing**

- **Canonical URL formatting** – how URLs are parsed and normalized (affects canonicalization, duplicate detection).  
- **HTTP response codes** – 2xx, 3xx, 4xx, 5xx semantics used by crawlers for indexing and error handling.  

**Key URLs:**  
- URL Standard: `https://url.spec.whatwg.org/`  
- HTTP/1.1 Semantics: `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110`  

---

### 10. Google Mobile‑Friendliness and Page Experience Guidance

(Portions now integrated into the main Search Central docs, but still canonical for testing criteria.)

**Name:** Mobile‑friendly & Page Experience guidance  
**Organization:** Google  

**What it defines / primary test categories**

- **Mobile‑friendliness criteria** – font size, viewport configuration, tap target sizing, horizontal scrolling avoidance.[1]  
- **Page experience signals** – Core Web Vitals, HTTPS, intrusive interstitial guidance, safe browsing.[1]  

These criteria are reflected in Lighthouse audits and in some Search Console reports.[4]  

**Key URL (entry point via Search Central):**  
- Page experience & mobile documentation is accessible from: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/`[1]  

---

If you want, I can next map these sources directly to a test‑plan matrix (rows = tools/standards, columns = categories: crawlability, indexing, mobile, metadata, canonicalization, structured data, social cards, etc.) for quick QA/SEO checklist building.
